text: This is a video of a robotic thymectomy that I performed. This is the full unedited video.
I've run it fast in certain areas in order to help with your viewing convenience.
A CT scan image of the patient's thymoma is displayed, with an arrow indicating the location of the abnormal tissue.
The video shows a thickened area of tissue with budding near the aorta, visible at the twelve o'clock position.
and the patient's had some symptoms in terms of shortness of breath and some weakness, some eye problems, and the treatment is surgical resection of the thymus.
The surgeon makes four incisions in the right chest, preparing for the robotic thymectomy to remove the thymus from the diaphragm to the lower neck, spanning from one phrenic nerve to the other.
The surgeon makes the first incision and inserts the robotic camera into the right chest, providing direct visualization for creating additional incisions.
The surgeon inserts an 8mm robotic port for the XI robot into the thoracic cavity, aiming to minimize trauma or injury to the surrounding thoracic structures.

The diaphragm is visible along the inferior portion of the surgical field. A third robotic port is introduced, followed by a larger, 12-millimeter assistant port positioned more laterally. These ports are utilized for the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon removes the video camera and positions it aside, with the robotic ports visible in their designated positions, preparing to dock the robotic apparatus to the surgical table.
The surgeon attaches the robotic arms to the previously inserted ports, followed by the insertion of the video camera and robotic instruments through these ports.
The video camera is reinserted through the robotic port, and the surgeon gains control using the robotic control panel.
The surgeon introduces a bipolar cautery instrument through the robotic port, designed to grasp tissues while transferring energy between its tips to cut and cauterize the tissue.
The surgeon prepares to engage the mediastinum, located at the center of the chest, using robotic instruments inserted through the established ports, minimizing thermal spread to surrounding tissues.
The surgeon deflects the lung downward while connecting CO-II to push the lung aside, adjusting the camera to provide a downward view of the surgical field.
The surgeon observes adhesions between the lung and the diaphragm, as well as the fat of the pericardium, using the enhanced view provided by the camera over the lung.
The surgeon deflects the lung without grasping it, carefully maneuvering to avoid injury or air leakage from the lung tissue.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes small adhesions to free the lung, allowing it to be displaced downward and out of the surgical field. The impulse of the heart is visible beneath the pericardium.
The surgeon identifies the whitish pericardium layer with visible tiny blood vessels, taking care to avoid opening the pericardium during the procedure.
The phrenic nerve is visible, coursing from left to right. The surgeon uses a bipolar cautery device to carefully approach the nerve without causing injury, ensuring the diaphragm remains unaffected.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes connective tissue and fat from the mediastinum, noting the thin, whitish appearance of the tissue, which is semi-transparent. The surgeon displaces and grasps the tissues without causing injury or air leaks.

The surgeon carefully dissects in the areolar tissue plane, avoiding major arteries and veins. Tiny blood vessels are visible, with minimal bleeding observed during the dissection, ensuring the specimen remains intact.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the pericardium, carefully avoiding the phrenic nerve, which is visible nearby. The aorta is located just below the area of dissection.
The surgeon extends the dissection toward the left side, maintaining the same plane around the specimen. The phrenic nerve is visible but remains uninjured as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon holds the thymectomy specimen, a mass, and continues to reposition and dissect it, moving back and forth to find the most effective method for removal. The surgical field is straightforward due to minimal fat involvement.

The surgeon continues to elevate the thin, translucent tissues, approaching the innominate vein that courses across the aorta. The superior vena cava is visible at the bottom portion of the screen, requiring careful manipulation to avoid injury.

The surgeon identifies the phrenic nerve as the border of the dissection area, with the innominate vein visible nearby. Using an instrument, the surgeon gently pushes the tissue without applying excessive cautery to avoid injuring the vein.

The robot is strong enough to tear tissues and to put holes in things if you're not careful, so that's part of becoming a facile and expert with the robot is learning how to maneuver it without injuring the structures. I can work close to the superior vena cava, the heart, the aorta, and all these structures without fear of injuring them. So I'm coming up now.

The surgeon dissects the outermost portion of the tissue, using cautery to take a small artery branch. A larger vein, identified as the mammary vein, is visible behind the artery, and care is taken not to disturb it.
The surgeon carefully dissects tissue strand by strand to expose the innominate vein, taking precautions to avoid bleeding while working directly above the vein.

The surgeon identifies a small vessel branch and dissects it down, revealing a branch of the innominate vein. Using gentle tissue manipulation, the surgeon cauterizes the small vein without using a clip, and prepares to divide the venous branch of the innominate vein as it extends into the specimen.

The surgeon cauterizes a vessel to prevent bleeding, then divides it to clear the surgical field. The dissection continues up and over the innominate vein, with connective tissue layers being carefully separated.

The surgeon continues to dissect tissue in the same plane, carefully pushing and dividing structures without excessive use of cautery. The specimen remains intact, with tissue being removed from the area between it and the pericardium.

The surgeon removes charred tissue to improve thermal conduction and adjusts the camera angle to visualize the lower neck region. The focus is on accessing and removing the two horns of the thymus that extend into the neck as part of the thymectomy procedure.

The surgeon moves closer to visualize a horn of the thymus extending behind a branch of the vein. The assistant uses an instrument to grasp and pull the specimen, allowing the surgeon to push the vein aside for better exposure into the lower neck. The surgeon prepares to divide the structures using cautery.

The surgeon adjusts the position of the instrument to reduce conflict with the assistant's instrument. The superior horn of the thymus is being dissected, with visible connective tissue aiding in identifying the dissection plane.

The surgeon carefully thins out the surrounding tissues to improve visibility, ensuring that no structures are injured or mistakenly cut. A branch of the vein is visible, which the surgeon avoids while continuing the dissection.

The surgeon continues dissection across loose connective tissues, focusing on the inferior portion toward the left side. Careful manipulation ensures the specimen is not compromised, while navigating the complex anatomy.

The surgeon identifies a vein near the specimen, noting their similar consistency. The camera is flipped for better visualization, allowing the surgeon to dissect loose connective tissue and expose the edge of the specimen. A small vessel is cauterized to control minor bleeding.

The surgeon identifies the branch of the vein near the superior vena cava (SVC) and realizes that the structure held is the innominate vein, not the specimen. The dissection continues to separate the specimen from the vein directly beneath the current working area.

The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical field, focusing on the plane needed to separate the specimen from the vein. The vein and specimen appear similar in consistency, requiring vigilance. The camera is flipped to improve visibility as the surgeon approaches the other horn of the thymus in the neck region.

The surgeon instructs the assistant to pull down on the vein to improve exposure, ensuring the instrument remains closed to minimize injury risk. The vein is deflected downward to open up the space and expose the tissue along the inferior border of the specimen.

The surgeon pulls up the connective tissue, revealing a stretched area that guides the dissection. A branch of the innominate vein is visible, with a smaller branch leading to the specimen. The surgeon clips, coagulates, and divides the branch, ensuring no bleeding occurs.

The surgeon divides the remaining connective tissue around the specimen, gently pushing off venous branches. The specimen is nearly fully circumscribed, and the surgeon maneuvers above the horn-like projection to completely free the specimen from its attachments.

The surgeon observes a large open space in the mediastinum and introduces a specimen retrieval bag. The specimen is placed into the bag, which is pulled out of the surgical field for pathological examination.
That will help guide prognostic information and also help determine if the patient needs an additional therapy, which oftentimes surgery is the definitive therapy.

The surgeon inserts a drain through one of the port sites after partially removing the specimen. The pleura on the left side appears bulged, suggesting trapped air, prompting the surgeon to make a small hole in the pleura and extend the chest tube into the left pleural space. The tube is positioned over the pericardium and attached to a collection chamber. The surgical instruments are removed, and the incisions are closed with local anesthetic for pain control.
The camera view shows the specimen appearing significantly smaller outside the chest, highlighting the effect of magnification used during the procedure.
